text: Trochars are inserted under direct guidance, followed by opening the pleura under the sternum using cautery. The surgeon employs a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques to dissect the thymus.
The surgeon uses scissors to divide the pleura near the phrenic nerve, revealing the junction of the mammary and innominate vein medial to the nerve.
The innominate vein is clearly visible as the surgeon uses blunt dissection to separate the thymus from the pericardium.
The surgeon dissects and divides the thymic veins draining into the innominate vein using a ligature device. The retroinnominate portion of the thymus is carefully dissected.

The surgeon dissects the left superior horn of the thymus away from the great vessels, followed by addressing the right superior horn. The dissection reveals retroinnominate extensions overlying the innominate vein.
The surgeon addresses the thymic tissue overlying the innominate vein, using dissection techniques to separate it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon dissects additional portions of the cervical thymus, maintaining continuity with the rest of the thymus, while dividing visible fascial attachments.

The surgeon retrieves the fully freed thymus gland and places it in a bag. A hole is created in the pleura to manage the contralateral capnothorax, and a valsalva maneuver is performed as the trochars are removed. The bag containing the thymus is then extracted from the surgical field.
